Handlers Effects & replay

rewindjs.com
Reference

Effects & replay

A handler is pure, but real apps talk to the world. The effect model is how outbound work happens without giving up determinism — which is what keeps every request replayable.

The invariant

Inside an activation your code is imperative, but the platform records every non-deterministic read so the activation can be re-run identically. The one rule that makes that hold:

An effect may return data to a handler only as a recorded message. Nothing reaches your code through a back channel; results come back as a new, taped activation.

So you never await a fetch and read its body inline. You start the fetch as an effect and handle its result in a separate export (onResult), where the response arrives as request.result — a recorded message. On replay, that message is served from the tape, not the network.

The four primitives

The whole engine, minus your handlers, is four primitives — two singletons and two open families. Every named effect is a composition of them.

PrimitiveIs
The Model (singleton)the durable, replicated kv — the only state that survives between activations
The Continuation (singleton)a held connection waiting to resume — what next() produces
Msg origins (open family)the ways an activation can start: inbound HTTP, a fetch result, a timer, a kv change, a webhook delivery…
Cmd runtimes (open family)the outbound effects: the one HTTP primitive, the kv writeset, timers…

Two corollaries fall out, and they're the reason replay is possible at all: inputs are durable (recorded as messages) and outputs are derivable (re-running the inputs reproduces them).

Durability is composed, not built in

There is one outbound primitive: a single HTTP send. The durable effects you actually call — webhook.send, email.send, retry.* — are JavaScript libraries that compose retry, idempotency, and at-least-once delivery on top of that primitive plus kv. The runtime doesn't special-case them; they're recipes, which means you can read them, and write your own.

// ephemeral: tied to this connection, dies if the caller leaves
on.fetch(url, opts);            // → resumes onResult with the taped response

// durable: a new request that runs whether or not anyone's connected
webhook.send({ url, body });    // retried to completion, survives restarts

Scope is the verb

Whether an effect is ephemeral or durable is decided by which verb you call, not a flag. A continuation's durability is forced by its scope: a connection-scoped hold is moot if the caller disconnects (so it needn't be persisted); a tenant-scoped effect must survive anything (so it is). The grammar position is the guarantee.

Ephemeral (connection)Durable (tenant)
Outputstream.write— (write to kv)
Triggeron.timer / on.fetch / on.kvschedule / cron / webhook.send

Why this is the product

Every other property — replay a production request, step a bug in a simulator, prove a fix against the exact recorded inputs — is downstream of these rules. Keep the handler pure and the effects recorded, and the platform can hand you a perfect reproduction of anything that ever ran. That's the whole bet.
